the descendents.rawNotes:
The Descendents are an American punk rock band formed in Manhattan Beach, California, in 1977, by guitarist Frank Navetta, bassist Tony Lombardo and drummer Bill Stevenson as a power pop/surf punk band.

 

In 1979, they enlisted Stevenson's school friend Milo Aukerman as a singer, and reappeared as a melodic hardcore punk band, becoming a major player in the hardcore scene developing in Los Angeles at the time.

 

Since 1986, the band's lineup has consisted of vocalist Milo Aukerman, drummer Bill Stevenson, guitarist Stephen Egerton, and bassist Karl Alvarez.





The initial trio recorded the band's debut single at Media Art studios and released it on their own label, Orca Records, named after Stevenson's fishing boat.

 

They next released the Fat EP in 1981 and their debut album Milo Goes to College in 1982 through New Alliance.

 

In 1985 Stevenson left Black Flag and the band reconvened for I Don't Want to Grow Up, recorded that April at Music Lab studios in Hollywood, Los Angeles, California.

 

Enjoy! was recorded in March and April 1986 at Radio Tokyo studios in Venice, Los Angeles.

 

All was recorded in January 1987 at Radio Tokyo studios in Venice, Los Angeles with recording engineer Richard Andrews.

 

Everything Sucks was recorded in June and July 1996 at The Blasting Room, a studio built and run by Stevenson in Fort Collins, Colorado.

 

Cool to Be You recording sessions took place in February 2002 at The Blasting Room in Fort Collins, Colorado, with additional recording done in April at Planet of Sound in Wilmington, Delaware.





The Descendents diverged from their punk peers perhaps because of Beach Boys influences and sang about topics that touched many young punks' lives like parents, food, girls and being rejected.

 

Steven Blush, author of American Hardcore: A Tribal History, remarked that their "cheeky love songs disguised as hardcore blasts became the most aped formula in rock."

 

Critics have cited that their earlier music style which reflected hardcore punk being influential to modern day skate punk and pop-punk.



One defining anecdote involves 

Milo actually going to college (he attended the University of California, San Diego) just like the title of their debut.

 

Navetta left the band during the recording of I Don't Want to Grow Up to become a fisherman in Oregon, and Lombardo left after the recording as touring was incompatible with his career as a postal carrier.

 A remarkable studio moment occurred when 

the Descendents recorded their own farts live in the studio for inclusion on the title track and "Orgofart" (the latter has no instrumentation, consisting entirely of the band members cheering each other on as they fart into recording equipment).

 Another significant moment came when 

a caricature of singer Milo Aukerman was created by Rodger Deuerlein, a classmate of Aukerman and drummer Bill Stevenson's at Mira Costa High School who taunted Aukerman by drawing comic strips and posters depicting him as the class nerd.

 This mascot would go on to appear on numerous album covers, becoming iconic to the band's identity.

the ramones.rawNotes:
The Ramones were an American punk rock band formed in the New York City neighborhood Forest Hills, Queens in 1974.

 

The original members were Joey Ramone (byname of Jeffrey Hyman), Johnny Ramone (byname of John Cummings), Dee Dee Ramone (byname of Douglas Colvin), and Tommy Ramone (byname of Thomas Erdelyi).

 

All members adopted pseudonyms ending with the surname Ramone, although none were biologically related; they were inspired by Paul McCartney, who used the stage name Paul Ramon when the Beatles were still calling themselves The Silver Beetles.

 

Known for helping establish the punk movement in the United States and elsewhere, the Ramones are often recognized as one of the first bands of the genre. Although they never achieved significant commercial success during their existence, the band is seen today as highly influential in punk culture.





In ripped jeans and black leather jackets, the Ramones made their reputation with almost-nonstop touring and energetic live performances, notably at New York City's CBGB club.

 

The Ramones made their CBGB debut on August 16. Legs McNeil, who cofounded Punk magazine the following year, later described the impact of that performance: "They were all wearing these black leather jackets. And they counted off this song…and it was just this wall of noise…. They looked so striking. These guys were not hippies. This was something completely new."

 

Their tour of England in 1976 proved a major inspiration for the punk movement in Britain, where they enjoyed greater commercial success than at home.





Ramones is the debut studio album by the American punk rock band Ramones, released on April 23, 1976, by Sire Records. In January 1976 the band took a break from their live performances to prepare for recording at Plaza Sound studio. Sessions began later that month and were completed within a week for $6,400.

 

Their next two albums, Leave Home and Rocket to Russia, were both released in 1977. Both were produced by Tommy and Tony Bongiovi, the second cousin of Jon Bon Jovi.

 

The band's following studio album, 1980's End of the Century, was their only record produced by Phil Spector and their most successful album commercially, peaking at #44 on the United States Billboard 200.





Dee Dee continued to count each song's tempo, originating the Ramones trademark "1, 2, 3, 4!" shout between songs.

 

Seymour Stein recounted: "I can remember going to the studio and [the Ramones] had got there three hours earlier" than he expected, showing their work ethic during the breakneck debut session.

 

Influenced by the rebelliousness of their contemporaries the New York Dolls and by 1960s pop music (especially bubblegum and surf music), the Ramones brought their back-to-basics approach

 to rock and roll. 

The songs on Ramones addressed several lyrical themes including violence, male prostitution, drug use, and Nazism.

 

Lyrically, the band expanded into topical subjects, like 1986's "Bonzo Goes to Bitburg," a reference to Ronald Reagan's ill-advised trip to a German war cemetery.

 

The Ramones performed 2,263 concerts, touring virtually nonstop for 22 years, and released fourteen studio albums. In 1996, after a tour as part of the Lollapalooza music festival, they played a farewell concert in Los Angeles and disbanded.

propagandhi.rawNotes:
Propagandhi is a Canadian punk rock and metal band formed in Portage la Prairie, Manitoba, in 1986 by guitarist Chris Hannah and drummer Jord Samolesky.

 

The band is currently located in Winnipeg, Manitoba, and completed by bassist Todd Kowalski and guitarist Sulynn Hago.

 

The band's breakthrough came in 1992 after opening for California punk band NOFX, and Fat Mike signed them to Fat Wreck Chords, releasing their now-classic debut How to Clean Everything (1993).

 

The band recorded their debut in Los Angeles.





Their debut How to Clean Everything (1993) established their initial sound in the skate punk tradition, while Less Talk, More Rock (1996) intensified the aggression with tighter riffs and confrontational delivery, and By Today's Empires, Tomorrow's Ashes (2001), the band broadened their sonic range, integrating varied dynamics and extended compositions.

 

Potemkin City Limits (2005) expanded their sonic palette while digging deeper into global politics, corporate imperialism, and environmental collapse.

 

Live From Occupied Territory: An Official Bootleg was released on September 4, 2007, documenting a full concert at The Zoo venue in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada, on July 19, 2003.

 

Failed States came out on September 4, 2012, under Epitaph Records.



The band emerged from the Canadian prairie DIY hardcore scene, 

with Less Talk, More Rock causing controversy due to the band's pro feminism and "gay positive" stance which clashed with the sexist and homophobic culture of the West Coast punk rock scene, after which bassist John K. Samson departed and formed The Weakerthans.

 

Founded by guitarist/vocalist Chris Hannah and drummer Jord Samolesky, the band quickly gained notoriety for their relentless energy, biting satire, and refusal to play nice within punk's status quo.

 

Chris Hannah briefly adopted the pseudonym Glen Lambert in the run-up to and immediate aftermath of the release of Potemkin City Limits, causing confusion among some fans, reviewers, and commentators; the band concluded the prank by announcing on August 14, 2006, that Glen Lambert had been dismissed and would be replaced by "former" member Chris Hannah.

 

Ramsey Kanaan, founder of the anarchist publishing company AK Press, appears on "A Public Dis-Service Announcement from Shell" as the voice of the petroleum multinational, with partial proceeds of the album donated to AK and other activist groups.





Both in their lyrics and activism, the band's members champion various left-wing causes and veganism; their music takes a vocal stance against human rights violations, sexism, racism, nationalism, homophobia, capitalism and religion.

 

Propagandhi's core ideological positions are grounded in anarchism, rejecting state authority and hierarchical institutions, consistently critiquing nationalism and imperialism, viewing nation-states as mechanisms of oppression.

 

They've stood in solidarity with Indigenous sovereignty movements, championed animal rights, and called out complacency both inside and outside the punk scene.

green day.rawNotes:
Green Day formed in Rodeo, California in 1987 with lead singer and guitarist Billie Joe Armstrong and bassist Mike Dirnt, with drummer Tré Cool (Frank Edwin Wright III) joining in 1990

. 

Armstrong's father died when he was ten, fragmenting his family, while Dirnt was born to a heroin-addicted mother and adopted by a Native American mother and white father who divorced when he was seven

. 

Before taking their current name in 1989, the band was Sweet Children and were part of the late 1980s/early 1990s Bay Area punk scene that emerged from 924 Gilman Street club in Berkeley

.



The band's early releases were with independent label Lookout Records, including their first album 39/Smooth (1990)

. 

After signing with Reprise Records, the band started working with Rob Cavallo, who produced Dookie, Insomniac, Nimrod, American Idiot and Uno/Dos/Tre

. 

Dookie was recorded over just over three weeks at Fantasy Studios in Berkeley during September and October 1993

. 

Released in 1994, Dookie was called "the most important pop-punk album of all time" with massive singles like "Longview," "Basket Case," and "When I Come Around"

. 

Dookie sold over 10 million copies in the US and 20 million worldwide

.



After unexpected success, the band released Nimrod in 1997, which represented a major musical departure including the acoustic folk single "Good Riddance (Time of Your Life)" that became a major international hit

. 

Three years later, the band recorded 20 complete tracks but the masters were stolen from the studio, so rather than re-record, they scrapped the album deciding it wasn't their best work

. 

The punk rock opera American Idiot was released in 2004 instead, stunning fans and critics

. 

Recording for American Idiot took place at Studio 880 in Oakland and Ocean Way Recording in Hollywood between 2003 and 2004

. 

Before American Idiot's creation, the band underwent band therapy after accusations from Dirnt and Cool that Armstrong was controlling and a show-off taking the limelight

.



Armstrong's lyrics touched on personal experiences including boredom, anxiety, mass murder, divorce, and masturbation

. 

American Idiot was inspired by the Bush Administration and features subjects like the War in Afghanistan ("Holiday"), American media ("American Idiot") and Armstrong's father's death from cancer ("Wake Me Up When September Ends")

. 

Around the time of their major label success, Green Day began feeling alienated by the northern California punk scene—not punk enough for traditional fans yet not accessible enough for conventional rock, so they forged their own unique musical path

.

.

.

sum 41.rawNotes:
Sum 41 is a Canadian rock band formed in Ajax, Ontario, in 1996.

 

The band has its origins in the band Kaspir, which was formed by Deryck Whibley and Grant McVittie in 1994, inspired by Weezer and Nirvana, until the drummer was replaced by Steve Jocz and the music became increasingly inspired by NOFX, particularly their 1991 album Ribbed.

 

The band chose the name "Sum 41" after looking at the calendar and counting how many days it had been from the start of their summer break to the day the Warped Tour was on and seeing that it was forty-one days.

 

The band's final lineup consisted of Deryck Whibley (lead vocals, guitars, keyboards), Dave Baksh (lead guitar, backing vocals), Jason "Cone" McCaslin (bass, backing vocals), Tom Thacker (guitars, keyboards, backing vocals), and Frank Zummo (drums, occasional backing vocals).





Their first studio album, "All Killer No Filler", followed in 2001 and achieved mainstream success, particularly through the single "Fat Lip", which reached number one on the Billboard Modern Rock Tracks chart.

 

It was recorded at Metalworks Studios in Mississauga, Ontario, and produced by Jerry Finn.

 

Their second album, "Does This Look Infected?" (2002), continued their commercial success and featured the singles "Still Waiting" and "The Hell Song".

 

The album, recorded at Avatar Studios in New York City, was produced by Greig Nori.

 

In 2004, the band travelled to the Democratic Republic of the Congo with War Child Canada to document the effects of civil conflict. During their stay, fighting resumed in the region, and the group were evacuated by United Nations peacekeepers. In recognition of one of the officers involved, they titled their next album "Chuck".

 

Chuck was recorded at Sound City Studios in Van Nuys, California, and produced by Greig Nori.



Sum 41 emerged as pivotal figures in early-2000s pop-punk and alternative rock. 

Several publications have noted their impact on punk rock and on popular music in general. In 2025, Cult MTL wrote: "Sum 41 has shaped the landscape of punk music, inspiring countless artists and fans.

 

Island Records, wanting to capitalize on the pop punk fad popularized by Green Day and Blink-182, signed Sum 41 in early 2000.

 

The band toured for much of the year, playing 300 shows in 2001, including opening for Blink-182 on tour before returning to the studio to record another CD.

 One standout anecdote: 

Grabbing the attention of record executives turned out to be easy, though, with their self-created press kit, which included video of some of the pranks they loved to pull. "The band made home videos for its own amusement, featuring remakes of action movies and footage of the members driving around, drenching pedestrians with Super Soakers. In late 1999, they edited the footage into a ten-minute montage with music and sent it off to a bunch of major labels."

 Another memorable moment: 

all band members and band manager Nori have "41" tattooed in red on their right arms.





Sum 41's music is often categorized as punk rock, pop punk, and alternative rock.

 Lyrically, the band explored themes ranging from youthful rebellion and social commentary to introspection and darker emotional terrain. 

The album featured a harder and more edgier sound, and the lyrics featured a more serious outlook.

 

The album also features timely lyrics that capture the chaotic and divisive political and social times, as the 2010s were coming to a close, giving the songs considerable depth.

new found glory.rawNotes:
New Found Glory formed in Coral Springs, Florida in 1997

, originally called A New Found Glory before shortening their name. 

The band currently consists of Jordan Pundik on lead vocals, Ian Grushka on bass, Chad Gilbert on guitar and backing vocals, and Cyrus Bolooki on drums.

 

The origins date back to 1997 when Jordan Pundik and Ian Grushka played together in bands like Inner City Kids and Flip 60

, and

After forming in 1997, New Found Glory released their debut studio album Nothing Gold Can Stay in 1999.

 

The band then released their self-titled major label debut in 2000, with the album's song "Hit or Miss" peaking at number 15 on the Alternative Songs chart.

 

In 2002, the band became mainstream with their album Sticks and Stones and the album's hit "My Friends Over You".

 

The group's popularity continued with their 2004 album Catalyst.



Recording-wise, 

sessions for their 2000 self-titled album were held at Elysian Fields Studios in Boca Raton, Florida, and mixed at Chapel Studios in Los Angeles, California.

 

They returned to the studio in April to record their eighth album, titled Radiosurgery with Neal Avron the producer having also produced their second, third and fourth albums.

Sticks and Stones showed growth in the group's music and lyrics, expanding from themes about girls and high school to themes of growing up and leaving home.



A key anecdote: 

The group appeared as the band in the summer movie American Pie 2 in 2001.

in 1998 the group won a South Florida Slammie Award, given to the best new band in Florida, which bolstered their popularity and increased their touring circle.

during the recording process of Listen Up!, Gilbert recorded his guitar parts while going through chemotherapy, with touring rhythm guitarist Dan O'Connor assisting in the studio, and Gilbert noting: "When I needed to double a guitar track and take a rest, he could help out with that. alkaline trio.rawNotes:
Alkaline Trio is an American punk rock band formed in Chicago in 1996, currently consisting of Matt Skiba on vocals and guitar, Dan Andriano on vocals and bass, and Tosh Peterson on drums.

 

Founded in late 1996 by Skiba, bassist Rob Doran, and drummer Glenn Porter, the band released its debut single "Sundials" in 1997.

 

Following the single's release, Doran departed and was replaced by Andriano, who became co-lead vocalist alongside Skiba.





The band recorded their EP For Your Lungs Only and debut studio album Goddamnit in 1998.

 

After releasing Maybe I'll Catch Fire in 2000, drummer Glenn Porter left and was replaced by Mike Felumlee for From Here to Infirmary in 2001, which saw significant exposure through singles "Stupid Kid" and "Private Eye."

 

Derek Grant, who joined after From Here to Infirmary, had previously played for The Suicide Machines among other bands.

 

Alkaline Trio is part of Chicago's large circle of punk and hardcore bands.

 

Skiba was previously in Jerkwater, Blunt, and Traitors, while Andriano played with Slapstick and Tuesday.





Early albums Goddamnit and Maybe I'll Catch Fire were recorded at Chicago's Atlas Studios with producer Matt Allison, capturing the band's formative sound without major label polish.

 

For their 2010 album This Addiction, the band returned to Chicago to record at Atlas Studios, where they had recorded their first three albums, working to go back to their roots.

 

For My Shame Is True, Dan Andriano confirmed the band would work with Bill Stevenson at the Blasting Room in Fort Collins.





The album Good Mourning was released in 2003 on Vagrant, reaching number one on Billboard's Top Independent Albums chart, with "We've Had Enough" becoming the band's first charting song in the United States.

 

This Addiction, released in 2010 through their newly formed label Heart & Skull in partnership with Epitaph Records, became their highest-charting album, reaching number eleven on the Billboard 200.





Their music is characterized by dark lyrics, catchy hooks, fast tempos, and recurring subjects of alcoholism, depression, shattered relationships, drugs, and death.

 

The album Goddamnit featured song lyrics touching on suicide, emotional breakdowns, and police brutality.

 

The band fuses dark romanticism, sharp wit, and melodic punk urgency, building their devoted following on emotionally charged records where confessional storytelling, serrated guitars, and memorable hooks collided.





In 2026, Skiba met drummer Tosh Peterson at Swingers for lunch to discuss him joining the band; Peterson's mom also attended, and when told later that Skiba's hospitality extended to Peterson bringing his mother, Peterson replied that he wouldn't want to play with them if he hadn't been welcome to bring her—Skiba called him "a monster behind a drum kit" and "a beautiful human being."

 

From 2015 to 2022, Skiba performed with rock band Blink-182, initially filling in for Tom DeLonge before becoming an official member and recording two full-length albums.

 

Before Goddamnit's release, band members could not give up their day jobs as bike messengers and record store clerks, but only after the album came out in 1998 could they focus solely on music.

the menzingers.rawNotes:
The Menzingers are an American punk rock band from Scranton, Pennsylvania, formed in 2006.

 

The band consists of Greg Barnett (vocals, guitar), Tom May (vocals, guitar), Eric Keen (bass) and Joe Godino (drums).

 

The band formed in the wake of Scranton ska-punk/pop punk bands Bob and the Sagets, featuring Tom May, Joe Godino, Eric Keen, and Kos Mos, featuring Greg Barnett.

 

Their first release was a self-titled demo tape made in 2006, which made its way onto many top ten lists of that year.





Their debut full length, A Lesson in the Abuse of Information Technology (released on Go-Kart Records), was produced by Jesse Cannon.

 

Jesse Cannon remarked "The Menzingers have it all — the songs, the aggression, the heart, and the passion that most only dream of."

 

In 2009, the band released a four-song EP titled "Hold On Dodge" through Red Scare Industries, their first vinyl release.

 

The band then worked with producer Matt Allison on their next full-length, Chamberlain Waits, which was issued in April 2010.

 

On May 17, 2011, they signed to Epitaph Records. Brett Gurewitz, founder of Epitaph Records and guitarist of Bad Religion said "These guys play the kind of pure punk rock that I grew up with. They are seriously talented songwriters and I'm happy to welcome them to the Epitaph family."





They released On the Impossible Past on February 20, 2012, and it was met with critical acclaim, going on to be voted "Album Of The Year" 2012 for both Absolutepunk.net and Punknews.org.

 

The band continued to grow and refine their sound, incorporating elements of earthy and earnest heartland rock into their sound, a trend that was evident on their 2014 album, Rented World.

 

For the 2017 follow-up, After the Party, the band reflected on hitting their thirties and reminisced about their younger days.

 

Hello Exile was released on October 4, 2019, on Epitaph Records and the band went on a U.S. headlining tour with Tigers Jaw and Culture Abuse.

 

On August 5, 2020, the band announced they would be releasing their 7th album entitled From Exile, which would be an acoustic reimagining of their previous album Hello Exile. The album was written, composed and recorded by the bands members in separate locations due to COVID-19 restrictions.

 

The band's seventh studio album Some of It Was True, released on October 13, 2023.



A pivotal anecdote came when 

Bill Stevenson offered the band high praise on a podcast shared on their Patreon in late 2021, stating, "You know what guys, you're a real band."

 Another memorable moment occurred when 

at the Fest VII in Gainesville, FL they were approached by Brendan Kelly of The Lawrence Arms and subsequently signed to Red Scare Industries.

 The band's live shows have become legendary, with 

lead singer Greg Barnett often not being heard over the noise of the crowd, and members of the crowd regularly ending up on the stage before diving back into the crowd.





The Menzingers craft heart-on-sleeve punk rock that finds poetry in everyday struggle, rooted in working class life and restless wanderlust, with lyrics pulled from late-night bar conversations and cross-country drives that turn personal nostalgia, regret, and resilience into sing-along moments.

Joyce Manor is an American punk rock band formed in Torrance, California, in 2008.

 

The band's current lineup consists of singer-songwriter and guitarist Barry Johnson; guitarist Chase Knobbe; and bassist/backing vocalist Matt Ebert.

Lyrically, there's also a sobering reflective quality the band first nailed on 2011 fan-favorite "Constant Headache." With songs like "I Know Where Mark Chen Lives" and the title track, the new record finds the band turning that wistful tone into a darker one as they re-examine the drug abuse and alcoholism from their past. It's a theme which formed unintentionally as Joyce Manor reflected on people they lost to both diseases in recent years.



A defining anecdote involves the band's initial recording sessions for Never Hungover Again.

Bad Religion is an American punk rock band formed in Los Angeles, California, in 1980.

 

The group originally included vocalist Greg Graffin, guitarist Brett Gurewitz, bassist Jay Bentley and drummer Jay Ziskrout.

 

Singer Greg Graffin is the band's only constant member, though fellow founding members Jay Bentley and Brett Gurewitz have also been with the band for most of their history

, with 

Brian Baker joining in 1994, and Mike Dimkich and Jamie Miller joining in 2013 and 2015 respectively.





The band's lyrics cover topics related to religion, politics, society, the media and science.

 

Musically, they are noted for their melodic sensibilities and extensive use of three-part vocal harmonies.

 

Gurewitz created Epitaph Records soon after their formation, and most of the band's albums have since been released through this label.

 

The band pioneered the punk rock revival movement of the 1990s, establishing a formula for California-based punk bands such as Green Day and Epitaph-signed acts the Offspring, NOFX and Rancid.





After gaining a large underground following and critical praise through their releases on Gurewitz's label Epitaph in the 1980s and early 1990s, Bad Religion experienced mainstream success after signing to the major-label Atlantic in 1993.

 

1982 saw the release of their first full-length album, How Could Hell be Any Worse?, which gained them a sizable following.

 

The band reformed in 1987 and released Suffer in 1988, cementing their comeback in the punk community. Not only is this album considered one of their best by most fans, but it is often credited with "saving" the Southern Californian punk rock scene.

 

No Control (1989) and Against the Grain (1990) further increased the band's popularity, followed by Generator (1992).

 

The album Stranger Than Fiction was released in September 1994 and was their most successful album at the time, scoring such hits as "Stranger Than Fiction", "Infected", and a re-recording of "21st Century (Digital Boy)".





Suffer was recorded at Westbeach Recorders, Hollywood, California between April and May 1988, while No Control was recorded at the same venue in June 1989.

 

Bad Religion released The Gray Race (1996), produced by former Cars frontman Ric Ocasek.

 An intriguing anecdote involves 

Gurewitz's departure not being on good terms, as he later accused the band of selling out for leaving Epitaph, and he recorded a song with his new band The Daredevils entitled "Hate You," reportedly directed towards Jay Bentley.

 Another telling moment: 

Only 12 people showed up to see the band introduce new material from Into the Unknown in 1983, which the band met with a great deal of negativity from fans who embraced their previous Hardcore Punk sound.

 

The band originated in a high school garage dubbed the "Hell Hole," where teenage founders rehearsed daily for over a year before releasing their self-titled debut EP in 1981.

Rise Against is an American punk rock band from Chicago, formed in 1999.

, with

Both albums were met with underground success, and in 2003 the band signed with the major label DreamWorks, which was absorbed by Geffen. Rise Against's major label debut Siren Song of the Counter Culture (2004) brought the band mainstream success.





Rise Against's popularity grew with the release of Endgame (2011), which peaked at number two on the Billboard 200. The band's seventh and eighth albums, The Black Market (2014) and Wolves (2017), continued the trend of commercial success, and both peaked with the top ten on the Billboard 200.



Recording has centered on trusted collaborators.

to mark the release of Nowhere Generation, the city's Office of the Mayor declared June 4th to be Rise Against Day In Chicago.

"The Bells," a song about retreading your past that specifically explores the ghosts of their hometown of Richmond. That night, the band brought out the bones of that song and just started playing it, like rapid fire around the room with Eric kind of playing his drum sticks on his lap.

Dead Kennedys formed in June 1978 in San Francisco, California, when East Bay Ray advertised for bandmates in the newspaper The Recycler.

 

The original band lineup consisted of East Bay Ray (Raymond Pepperell) on lead guitar, Klaus Flouride (Geoffrey Lyall) on bass, Jello Biafra (Eric Reed Boucher) on vocals, Ted (Bruce Slesinger) on drums and 6025 (Carlos Cadona) on rhythm guitar.

 

Their first live show was on July 19, 1978 at Mabuhay Gardens in San Francisco.

 

6025 left the band in 1979, and Ted departed in 1980 after the recording of their debut album.

 

D. H. Peligro became the band's drummer in 1981, remaining their longest-serving drummer until his death in 2022.

 

Dead Kennedys were one of the most popular and important American hardcore punk bands of the late '70s/early '80s.





In June 1980, the band recorded their debut album, Fresh Fruit for Rotting Vegetables, released in September of that year on the UK label Cherry Red.

 

The album was recorded at Möbius Music in San Francisco, California, in June 1980 and was produced by East Bay Ray and the band.

 

Dead Kennedys released the EP In God We Trust Inc. (1981), followed by the studio albums Plastic Surgery Disasters (1982), Frankenchrist (1985), and Bedtime for Democracy (1986).

 

Live at the Deaf Club was recorded on March 3, 1979, at the Deaf Club in San Francisco.

 

Mutiny on the Bay is a live compilation album, featuring performances recorded at various locations around the San Francisco Bay Area between 1982 and 1986.





Holiday in Cambodia is perhaps the band's definitive moment – a perfect mix of hilarious yuppie-baiting lyrics and evil-sounding music.

 

Dead Kennedys' early success led them to record their first single, California Über Alles, a blistering attack on the then-governor of California, Jerry Brown.

 

On March 25, 1980, Dead Kennedys were invited to perform at the Bay Area Music Awards in an effort to give the event some new wave credibility. The day was spent practicing California über alles. After about 15 seconds into the song, Biafra stopped the band.

 

In the mid-1980s, the band was embroiled in an obscenity trial in the United States over the artwork of their album Frankenchrist (1985), which included the explicit titular subject of H.R. Giger.

 

Biafra was charged with distributing harmful matter to minors, a charge which he repulsed based on the First Amendment right to free speech and which was dismissed the following year.





In 1998, the other three members of the band sued Biafra over allegedly unpaid royalties. A jury ruled in their favor in May 2000, finding Biafra and Alternative Tentacles guilty of malice, oppression and fraud.

 

Biafra dismissed the new group as the world's greediest karaoke band.

 

Dead Kennedys' lyrics were usually political in nature, satirizing political figures and authority in general, as well as popular culture and even the punk movement itself.

 

Jello Biafra's biting lyrics tackled the sociopolitical concerns of the Reagan era with a distinct sense of morbid humor and satire, following in the footsteps of such earlier rock satirists as Frank Zappa and non-musical countercultural figures like Abbie Hoffman and Lenny Bruce.

Goldfinger is an American punk rock band from Los Angeles, formed in 1994.

 

The band was formed by former Electric Love Hogs member John Feldmann on vocals and guitar, Simon Williams on bass, Darrin Pfeiffer on drums, and Charlie Paulson on guitar.

 

Feldmann met bassist Simon Williams in a shoe store where they both worked, and the two made a demo recording to recruit drummer Darrin Pfeiffer.

 

Pfeiffer had recently moved from Buffalo, New York, to pursue music and was working at a Los Angeles coffee shop while playing in another band, but quit that band after listening to the demo.





In their early years, the band was considered a contributor to the movement of third-wave ska, as evidenced in their first three albums: Goldfinger (1996), Hang-Ups (1997) and Stomping Ground (2000).

 

The debut studio album was released on Mojo Records in February 1996 and produced by Mojo founder Jay Rifkin.

 

The releases of Open Your Eyes (2002) and Disconnection Notice (2005) saw the band shed most of the ska influence, and they have been more commonly placed in the pop-punk genre in later years.

 

The band has since released four more albums: Hello Destiny... (2008), The Knife (2017), Never Look Back (2020), and Nine Lives (2026).





"Here in Your Bedroom" became a hit after receiving extensive airplay on Los Angeles area rock station KROQ-FM.

 

The song was based on Feldmann's personal experience when he had a crush on a woman working in the dress department at the shoe store for over eight months, expressing his emotions as "a sensitive guy" in that situation.

 

In 1996, the band broke the Guinness Book of World Records for most shows ever played by a touring band with 385 shows played.





John Feldmann has been the sole constant member of the group.

 

Other longtime members have included Charlie Paulson (guitar), Simon Williams (bass), Kelly LeMieux (bass) and Darrin Pfeiffer (drums).

 

The band is also noted for their support for animal rights.

 

Feldmann stopped eating meat when he was 29 years old after watching the movie Babe and later became a vegan after four years of vegetarianism.

 

The songs "Free Me," "For Your Greed," and "What Gives You the Right" all promote animal rights.

 

Feldmann has produced records for Mest, the Used, Hilary Duff and Story of the Year.

, a place that would become their cultural incubator.

Stay Sick! is emblematic of The Cramps' signature style.

The combination of the two female musicians Poison Ivy and Miriam Linna made the Cramps unique in the testosterone-fueled Greenwich Village punk scene, with their campy theatrical excess and ear-splitting sonics giving them an edge the more cerebral bands couldn't muster.

performing at The Napa State Mental Hospital, a real mental facility they somewhat tricked into letting them play by telling managers they played cover songs.

Shelton Hank Williams, known professionally as Hank Williams III, is an American singer, songwriter, and multi-instrumentalist

 born December 12, 1972, 

in Nashville Tennessee

. Though not technically a punk band—more accurately a genre-defying solo artist anchored by multiple acts—the punk scene remains central to his legacy. 

He was the drummer of hardcore punk band Arson Anthem, and bassist of Phil Anselmo's band Superjoint Ritual

. 

Arson Anthem would be founded in 2006. After losing all his possessions in the aftermath of Hurricane Katrina, Mike Williams moved into Anselmo's spare apartment. The two spent countless hours listening to Phil's collection of early hardcore bands. They began jamming with Hank III and Collin Yeo, who were united by their desire to have a hardcore punk band of their very own

. 

According to Williams, the group spent six days in Houston, Texas recording eight songs in the spring of 2006

.



Williams spent much of his early career playing drums in punk rock bands during the late 1980s and early-to-mid-1990s

. The formation of his solo career emerged from necessity: 

Williams was informed that he had fathered a son, Coleman Finchum, who was five years old by that time; a family court judge ordered Williams to find more stable employment so that Finchum could receive child support

. 

Williams' first solo album, Risin' Outlaw, was released in September 1999 to respectable sales and strong reviews, despite Williams's own hatred of the record

. 

The album features instrumentation from Nashville studio veterans Jason Brown on upright bass, J. T. Corenflos and Brent Rowan on acoustic guitars, and Dan Dugmore, Kayton Roberts, and Paul Franklin on steel guitars

.

Key albums include 

Straight to Hell on Curb's rock imprint, Bruc. Battles with Walmart delayed the appearance of this album, which was released on February 28, 2006, as a two-disc set in two formats: a censored version (for Wal-Mart), and an uncensored version that was the first major-label country album ever to bear a parental advisory warning

. 

Williams released Assjack's self-titled debut album on August 4, 2009, through Curb

. 

Ghost to a Ghost/Gutter Town (a 2-disc country record with some ambient and folk influences), 3 Bar Ranch Cattle Callin' (a metal record in the newly anointed cattle core genre) and Attention Deficit Domination (a doom-rock record), were released under his label Hank3 Records through Megaforce Records, and featured guest appearances by Tom Waits, Les Claypool (Primus), Alan King (Hellstomper), Ray Lawrence Jr., Troy Medlin (Sourvein), Dave Sherman (Earthride) and Williams' dog, Trooper

.

An iconic anecdote: 

In the late 1980s, upon first meeting Hank Williams III, Minnie Pearl, a friend of Hank Williams Sr., reportedly said "Lord, honey, you're a ghost", as she was astonished by his striking resemblance to his grandfather

. Another moment came via court summons: 

Three years after a one night stand in 1995, Hank Williams III was served papers on stage while opening up for the legendary underground band Buzzov•en. The judge told Hank 3 that playing music was no real job and to come up with $60,000 in overdue child support

.



His live shows typically follow a Jekyll and Hyde format: a country music set featuring fiddle player David McElfresh and steel guitar player Dan Johnson, followed by a "hellbilly" set of cowpunk and psychobilly songs, and then an Assjack set, which consists of death metal and metalcore songs

. 

Williams' lyrical themes include drug use, hedonism and the outlaw life, as well as criticism of the mainstream country music industry

.

dead milkmen.rawNotes:
The Dead Milkmen formed in Philadelphia in 1983 with original members Rodney Linderman (Rodney Anonymous) on vocals and keyboards, Joe Genaro (Joe Jack Talcum) on guitar and vocals, Dave Schulthise (Dave Blood) on bass, and Dean Sabatino (Dean Clean) on drums

. 

The band's concept began in 1979 as Genaro's home-recording project in Chester County, Pennsylvania, where he and high school friend Garth created an imaginary band with a fictional backstory, recording homemade cassettes—the moniker coming from character Milkman Dead in Toni Morrison's Song of Solomon

.



The band attracted college radio attention with their 1985 debut album Big Lizard in My Backyard on Restless Records, with "Bitchin' Camaro" becoming especially popular

. 

The band enjoyed international success with "Punk Rock Girl" from their 1988 Beelzebubba album, which entered MTV rotation

. 

Their discography included Eat Your Paisley! (1986), Bucky Fellini (1987), Metaphysical Graffiti (1990), Soul Rotation (1992), Not Richard, But Dick (1993), and Stoney's Extra Stout (Pig) (1995)

. 

For their 2023 album Quaker City Quiet Pills, the band recorded at Sine Studios in Philadelphia

.



The band distinguished itself in the hardcore punk scene through its jangly punk sound and sardonic humor delivered with thick Philadelphia accents

. 

Topics for their music included satire on the 1980s music scene, toxic waste, jellyfish afterlifes, UFOs, conspiracy theories, and cover bands

. 

The song "Bitchin' Camaro" remained a favorite at live shows because of its improvised dialogue intro

. 

The 1988 release of Beelzebubba marked a commercial breakthrough propelled by "Punk Rock Girl," which secured MTV rotation including a chaotic live performance on Club MTV that showcased the band's irreverent humor and stage antics

. 

After an ill-fated stint with Hollywood Records, relations between band and label soured, and the group smuggled an unlisted version of Soul Rotation's "If I Had a Gun" onto their 1994 live album Chaos Rules: Live at the Trocadero since the label refused permission to feature Hollywood Records songs on compilations

. 

In November 2004, the Dead Milkmen played two consecutive nights at the Trocadero Theatre in Philadelphia in memory of Dave Schulthise, with proceeds donated to mental health organizations and a Serbian monastery he supported

.

hot water music.rawNotes:
Hot Water Music is an American punk rock band formed in October 1994 and based in Gainesville, Florida.

 

The group has consisted of Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard on shared lead vocals and guitars, bass guitarist Jason Black, and drummer George Rebelo.

 

The band's name was taken from a collection of short stories by Charles Bukowski of the same name.

 

Frustrated by the lack of musical opportunities in their respective hometowns, Black recommended that the other three join him in Gainesville, a city known for its historic and contemporary music scene. Upon moving to Gainesville and variously filling in as performers in each other's bands Fossil and Thread, the four joined as Hot Water Music in October 1994.





After rapidly releasing a series of singles splits, and EPs, the band released their first two LPs in 1997, Fuel for the Hate Game and Forever and Counting.

 

At least three albums—1999's No Division, 2001's A Flight And A Crash and 2002's Caution—would serve not only as lynchpin albums not just in the band's career but in the post-punk or melodic hardcore or post-hardcore genre itself.

 

On January 23, 2012 the band announced via their official Twitter page that they had set up camp at The Blasting Room in Ft. Collins, Colorado to record their new full-length album with Bill Stevenson producing.

 The Blasting Room became integral to Hot Water Music's later work, producing their 2012 album Exister and other reunion-era releases.



The band's rigorous touring exhausted the members and strained their personal relationships. Tensions came to a head on a European tour and Hot Water Music decided to break up for the sake of preserving their friendships with each other. After finishing the rest of their dates in Europe and returning to Florida, they spent three months apart and then reconvened for rehearsals for a farewell show. The rehearsals went unexpectedly well, so much so that the band wound up writing four new songs. By the time they performed the gig, in Gainesville on August 28, 1998, the band had settled their differences.

 

During the show, Ragan announced to the audience, "I'm sure a lot of people here came a long way tonight because they heard this was our last show. I don't know what to tell you guys...we're not keeping it going." The performance from this show was recorded and released as the live album Live at the Hardback.





The band initially broke up in August 1998, but reformed by October 1998.

 

The group split up again in 2006, but has remained active since 2008.

 

A hallmark of their approach is the dual male vocals of Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard, delivered in a gritty, call-and-response format that amplifies themes of personal struggle, community, and perseverance through overlapping shouts and harmonies.

 Their lyrics consistently emphasize resilience, working-class identity, and emotional vulnerability—themes that resonated deeply with the post-hardcore community and solidified their influence across multiple generations of punk and emo bands.

bouncing souls.rawNotes:
The Bouncing Souls are an American punk band from New Brunswick, New Jersey, formed in 1989.

 

The group comprises Greg Attonito (lead vocals), Pete Steinkopf (guitar), Bryan Kienlen (bass), and George Rebelo (drums).

 

The four original members grew up in Basking Ridge, New Jersey, and played in smaller bands while attending Ridge High School.

 

They made the decision to move to a college town; New Brunswick, NJ, which is the home of Rutgers University.

 

The band's name is a derivative of Doc Martens shoes, specifically their advertising slogan "with Bouncing Soles".





Their first full-length album, The Good, The Bad & The Argyle, was released in 1994 on Chunksaah Records, their own label.

 

Two tracks of this record, "Candy" and "What Boys Like" served as homage to 1980s new wave while simultaneously striking a humorous jab at homophobia.

 

Their next album, Maniacal Laughter, was released in 1996, with half the album written in a week.

 

It led to a high-profile tour with Youth Brigade that got the attention of Epitaph Records, and the Bouncing Souls signed with Epitaph in 1997 and released The Bouncing Souls later that year.

 

Hopeless Romantic followed in 1999 and has been heralded for admixing poignant lyrics with their melodic rapidly paced punk style.

 Other key albums include 

How I Spent My Summer Vacation (2001), Anchors Aweigh (2003), The Gold Record (2006), Ghosts on the Boardwalk (2010), and Comet (2012).





New Brunswick had a reputation for supporting underground music, and the Bouncing Souls not only became a staple in the New Brunswick music scene, but also helped other bands gain an audience by opening up for them in the clubs around town, as well as parties and shows they put on themselves.

 

By the time they were recognized by the national punk scene, they had reignited a "pogo" element to New Jersey punk rock by playing fast light-hearted songs, a model followed by various other local bands.



An anecdote from their early days comes via founding guitarist Pete Steinkopf, who recalled that 

"we were just a band that played our friends' backyards."

 Another notable moment came when 

in 2008, while at the Vans Warped Tour stop in their home state of New Jersey, the band helped Music Saves Lives raise the blood supply by signing a guitar for the non-profit to auction off.

 

After Hopeless Romantic, personal problems arose between the band and long-time drummer Shal Khichi which resulted in his departure from the band, and he was replaced by former Skinnerbox, Mephiskapheles, and Murphy's Law drummer, Michael McDermott.





The band's lyrics mix poignant themes with melodic punk delivery.

 

Songs range from humorous openings like "I like your mom and it's no fad, I wanna marry her and be your dad," to heartfelt material, alongside hometown pride anthems like "East Coast! Fuck You!"

 This blend of silly and sincere storytelling has anchored their thirty-plus year run, establishing them as foundational figures in Jersey punk culture.

against me!.rawNotes:
Against Me! formed in 1997 in Naples, Florida, founded by singer and guitarist Laura Jane Grace, who moved to Gainesville that same year, establishing it as the band's hometown.

 

Since 2001, guitarist James Bowman has been a consistent member alongside Grace.

 

The core lineup includes bassist Andrew Seward and drummer Atom Willard.

 

The band's name reflected Grace's feeling at that point: "The whole world is against me!"



The group evolved from acoustic folk-punk demos into a full electric band, releasing 

Reinventing Axl Rose on No Idea Records, followed by As The Eternal Cowboy and Searching for a Former Clarity on Fat Wreck Chords.

 

Against Me! moved to Sire Records for 2007's New Wave, which reached number 57 on the Billboard 200.

 

White Crosses arrived in 2010 and became their highest-charting album, supported by singles including "I Was a Teenage Anarchist" and "White Crosses."

 

In September 2011, Grace built a recording studio in Elkton, Florida, which was named Total Treble Studio.





After lineup turbulence, their sixth album Transgender Dysphoria Blues released independently in January 2014 to critical and commercial acclaim.

 

Their seventh full-length, Shape Shift with Me, arrived in September 2016.

 A pivotal moment arrived when 

"I felt like I drop-kicked them in the face," Grace said of informing the other members when she came out as transgender in May 2012, adding "We had the most awkward hug ever, and then they left."

 

Gender dysphoria became the theme of Transgender Dysphoria Blues, an autobiographical account of Grace's experience as a trans woman and of social and body dysphoria.

 Another striking detail: 

drummer Warren Oakes left in June 2009 to focus on running his Mexican food restaurant, emphasizing it "was really a mutual decision ... it was really amicable."



Lyrically, 

Against Me! explored personal rebellion and disillusionment, with "I Was a Teenage Anarchist" reminiscing about Grace's real-life teenage years as a punky rebel.

 

Grace's lyrics remained entrenched in protest against mainstream authority, continuing anti-establishment tradition throughout the band's career.

face to face.rawNotes:
Face to Face is a punk rock band from Victorville, California, formed in 1991 by frontman Trever Keith, bassist Matt Riddle and drummer Rob Kurth.

 

Keith and Riddle, who had been friends since high school, initially formed a heavy metal band named Victoria Manor

 before 

Keith and Riddle formed a band named Zero Tolerance with drummer Rob Kurth and guitarist Mark Haake who were both Air Force members stationed at George Air Force Base outside of Victorville.

 

By 1991, the band decided to take a new musical direction and changed their name to Face to Face, shortly afterwards Mark Haake left the band and the remaining members continued on as a three-piece.





The band rose to fame with their 1995 album Big Choice, featuring the radio hit "Disconnected" which received heavy rotation on KROQ radio.

 Key albums in their catalog include 

Don't Turn Away (1992), which helped the band build a reputation in the punk scene

, as well as 

a self-titled album in 1996 that gained further popularity through tours with bands like Sublime and NOFX.

 

They released Ignorance Is Bliss (1999) and Reactionary (2000) before guitarist Chad Yaro left the band in 2000.

 

They continued as a three-piece and recorded How to Ruin Everything (2002).



For recording venues, 

Face to Face entered Westbeach Recorders studio located in Hollywood, CA in October 1991 to record songs for their first album, Don't Turn Away (1992).

 

They later recorded sessions with Jim Goodwin who offered free studio time, and they recorded "Nothing New," "Pastel," and "Disconnected".

 

For Big Choice, Face to Face entered the studio with producer Thom Wilson (of The Offspring fame) and began recording.





The group began to move toward a more punk sound while retaining their earlier modern rock sensibilities.

 

The band's albums often feature themes of disillusionment, personal struggle, and social commentary, with singles like "Disconnected" and "Everything's Your Fault" gaining significant radio and MTV airplay.

 An interesting detail emerges in the label's testing strategy: 

After finishing Big Choice, Victory Music decided they wanted to test their distribution system with a new release by assembling an EP of songs from several 7" records along with other unreleased recordings, titled Over It and released several weeks before Big Choice, which contained a remixed version of "Disconnected" that began to receive significant airplay from KROQ.

 Another key anecdote: 

The recording of Don't Turn Away finished in just 2 weekends but faced trouble getting released.

 

When replacing original drummer Rob Kurth, the band auditioned 25 different drummers before finally finding Pete Parada.

the lawrence arms.rawNotes:
The Lawrence Arms are an American punk rock band from Chicago, formed in 1999.

 

The lineup consists of Brendan Kelly (vocals & bass), Chris McCaughan (vocals & guitar), and Neil Hennessy (drums).

 

Brendan Kelly had played in the ska punk band Slapstick, Chris McCaughan had played in Tricky Dick before joining Kelly in the punk band the Broadways, and Neil Hennessy had played in a band called Baxter.

 

The three musicians came together to form the Lawrence Arms in 1999, taking their name from the apartment complex in which Kelly and McCaughan had lived before being evicted in the middle of the night.





Although punk rock bands in their infancy generally start out by releasing EPs and 7-inch vinyl singles, the band members used their existing relationship with Asian Man Records to immediately begin recording a full-length album, A Guided Tour of Chicago, which was released later that year and before the band had even played a single show. A second album, Ghost Stories, followed in 2000. Both albums focused heavily on the members' own stories of growing up and living in Chicago.

 

Their first release to be recorded by Matt Allison, who would continue to record and produce all of their subsequent releases, came in 2000 as a split with Shady View Terrace.

 

This was followed by the full-length album Apathy and Exhaustion, released in 2002. Their next album was 2003's The Greatest Story Ever Told. Their most ambitious album to date, it demonstrated the band members' continued growth as musicians and songwriters and included extensive footnotes to the lyrics detailing their many references to history, literature and pop culture.

 

The Lawrence Arms re-entered the studio in October and November 2005 and recorded the album Oh! Calcutta!, which was released on Fat Wreck Chords in March 2006. The album was noted as faster and more energetic than the band's prior releases, with Kelly and McCaughan sharing lead vocals on every track.





Fat Mike of NOFX signed them to his Fat Wreck Chords label in late 2001.

 The Chicago scene shaped them deeply through the '90s and into the aughts; 

they contributed a song to the Fat Wreck Chords Rock Against Bush, Vol. 2 compilation, leading up to the 2004 presidential election and became involved with the Punk Voter campaign. The Lawrence Arms also played a portion of the 2004 Warped Tour, but the band's attitude towards the tour resulted in controversy.

 

In 2002, the Lawrence Arms got booted from the Warped Tour after bashing the festival organizers onstage for allowing concession stands to sell bottled water for more than $5. As a hidden bonus track on the new album, "Warped Summer Extravaganza (Major Excellent)" offers a scathing account of that experience.

 More broadly, 

Kelly criticized the tour's effect on punk rock and independent music as a whole: "Now people talk about the Warped Tour like it's the greatest thing to ever happen to punk rock, it's not the greatest thing to ever happen to punk rock - it's single handedly dismantling the whole thing we've been fucking building for all this time and nobody gives a fuck."





Their songs often weave dark humor, personal mythology and sharp observations into anthems that feel both intimate and communal, with beauty in chaos and camaraderie in singalong choruses.

 

Their music and lyrics exhibit their propensity for juxtaposing references from history and literature with American pop culture.

 

Kelly has said "We all have a lot of experiences -- good ones and terrible ones -- that are influenced by us being influenced by alcohol. That's what we write about." Kelly also has a lot to say about politics, and he always says what he thinks.
